eastbound.primary: A side (left/north) — Rainier, Hanford, Snake River Plain
eastbound.secondary: C/D side (right/south) — Adams, St. Helens, Oregon high desert

westbound.primary: C/D side (right/south) — Rockies climbout, afternoon Cascades
westbound.secondary: A side (left/north) — Wyoming basin, Snake River

systemPrompt.role: You are an expert aerial guide with deep knowledge of geology, history, ecology, and human geography. The passenger is looking out their window at 28,000-35,000 feet. Your job is to make what they see feel alive — not a tour script, but a genuine conversation that follows their curiosity. Always offer a divergent path. Never give a dead end. If the passenger asks 'what's that?' you have context below to anchor your response. Current position, altitude, heading, and seat side are in the context. Use them.

waypoints.name: Duwamish River Industrial Corridor
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: One of the most contaminated Superfund sites in the US is directly below on rotation. Boeing built the B-17 here. The river itself was straightened by hand in 1913 — workers physically moved the entire channel to make room for industry.
divergentPaths: Boeing B-17 production and WWII air war
Superfund cleanup process and what that actually means
Duwamish tribe — still federally unrecognized despite being Seattle's original inhabitants
How rivers get straightened and what it does to ecosystems

waypoints.name: Mt. Rainier North Flank
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: The most topographically prominent peak in the lower 48 — more vertical rise from its base than any other mountain, including Everest measured the same way. There are 26 named glaciers. The Carbon Glacier is the lowest-elevation glacier in the contiguous US. Paradise weather station once held the world record for annual snowfall.
divergentPaths: Lahar risk — flow paths cover Puyallup and Orting suburbs, essentially zero evacuation time
USGS volcano monitoring network and what the sensors actually measure
The 26 glaciers and accelerating melt rates
Why prominence vs height is a more honest measure of a mountain

waypoints.name: Yakima Valley
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: This valley produces roughly 75% of all US hops. Every IPA you have ever had probably has Yakima in it. The valley sits at the same latitude as Burgundy, France — which is why the wine coming out of here is genuinely world-class and not a regional novelty.
divergentPaths: How hops are grown and why this specific valley dominates
Yakima wine appellations and how they compare to European equivalents
Rain shadow effect — how the Cascades create this desert at this latitude
Apple production — Yakima is also the apple capital of the US

waypoints.name: Hanford Site
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: 56 million gallons of radioactive waste sit in aging underground tanks directly below. This site produced the plutonium for the Trinity test and Fat Man — the bomb dropped on Nagasaki. The B Reactor is now a national historic landmark you can actually tour. It is the only place on earth where you can stand next to the machine that produced the material that ended World War II.
divergentPaths: The Manhattan Project — who built Hanford and how fast
The cleanup — ongoing since 1989, projected completion 2077, most contaminated nuclear site in Western hemisphere
B Reactor tour — what you actually see and why it's worth going
The workers — most had no idea what they were building

waypoints.name: Columbia-Snake River Confluence
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: Lewis and Clark camped exactly at this confluence in October 1805. For thousands of years before that it was one of the largest trading hubs in North America — dozens of tribes converging to trade salmon, camas root, and obsidian. The scale of those gatherings rivaled any market in the contemporary world.
divergentPaths: Lewis and Clark — what they actually found here and how they were received
Celilo Falls — the most productive fishery in North America, now drowned by The Dalles Dam in 1957
Camas root economy and how it sustained entire civilizations
The dams — 4 lower Snake River dams and the ongoing salmon restoration debate

waypoints.name: Snake River Plain
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: The entire flat plain below is the scar of the Yellowstone hotspot moving northeast over millions of years. The North American plate is drifting over a fixed plume of magma. What you're looking at is what's left after the hotspot burned through. The magma chamber currently under Yellowstone will eventually produce the next eruption — and the plain shows exactly where it has been.
divergentPaths: Yellowstone supervolcano — actual eruption probability vs public perception
How hotspot tracks work and what other ones exist globally
The Bonneville Flood — ancient megaflood that carved much of this landscape
Why Idaho is America's largest trout producer — the aquifer fed by this geology

waypoints.name: Shoshone Falls / Twin Falls
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: Shoshone Falls drops 45 feet higher than Niagara. Almost no one outside Idaho has heard of it. During irrigation season, so much water is diverted upstream that the falls can be reduced to a trickle. The same river that carves one of the most dramatic waterfalls in North America gets essentially turned off for agriculture.
divergentPaths: Why Niagara is famous and Shoshone isn't — tourism history and railroad marketing
Western water rights — prior appropriation doctrine and what it means
Evel Knievel's 1974 Snake River Canyon jump attempt — it happened right here
The Snake River Canyon geology — carved by the same Bonneville megaflood

waypoints.name: Craters of the Moon
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: NASA trained Apollo astronauts here because it is the closest terrain on Earth to the lunar surface. The last eruption was roughly 2,000 years ago. In geologic terms that is this morning — this field is not finished.
divergentPaths: Apollo training program — which astronauts trained here and what they learned
Lava tube caves beneath the surface — some still uncharted
Why basaltic lava fields look lunar and what that tells us about the Moon's formation
The next eruption — what USGS monitoring shows

waypoints.name: Rock Springs / Wyoming
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: The dark seams cut into the hills below are coal — and they're the only reason Rock Springs exists. Union Pacific didn't build a town near coal; they built a town to mine coal to feed their own trains. The railroad created a city just to feed itself.
divergentPaths: Chinese railroad workers — the full history of who built the transcontinental railroad and what they received for it
The Page Act and Chinese Exclusion Act — how the massacre fed into federal policy
Rock Springs today — coal industry decline and what replaced it
The survivors — what happened to the Chinese community after the massacre

waypoints.name: Rawlins / Tom Horn Country
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: Below you is the country where Tom Horn worked as a hired gun for the cattle barons — and where he was hanged in 1903 for a killing he may not have committed. He was one of the last men executed by a drop-and-lever gallows he helped design himself, for a different prisoner. The frontier was closing fast enough that Horn outlived the era that made him useful, and it killed him for it.
divergentPaths: Tom Horn — Pinkerton detective, Army scout, hired range detective, killer
The range wars — cattle barons vs homesteaders and what was actually at stake
Wyoming's frontier justice system and how it differed from eastern courts
The Johnson County War — the event that made Horn necessary, from the cattle barons' perspective

waypoints.name: Hells Canyon
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: The gorge below is deeper than the Grand Canyon, and almost nobody's heard of it. The Snake River carved it, and it happens to mark the exact line where Idaho and Oregon quietly touch — a state border most passengers fly over without knowing it's there.
divergentPaths: Why Hells Canyon stayed obscure while the Grand Canyon became iconic
The Snake River's full carving history across three states
Idaho-Oregon border geography and how state lines follow rivers
Wildlife in the canyon — bighorn sheep and one of the last wild river stretches

waypoints.name: Massacre Rocks
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: Despite the name, nothing was massacred at Massacre Rocks. Pioneers named it out of fear after a skirmish that happened somewhere else entirely — and the wrong name just stuck for 160 years. Sometimes the scariest story about a place is the one that never actually happened there.
divergentPaths: How the naming myth spread and why it never got corrected
The actual 1862 skirmish that inspired the fear
Oregon Trail landmarks that got their names wrong
The real Massacre Rocks State Park — what's there today

waypoints.name: Bear Lake
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: That impossibly blue water below shouldn't exist in high desert — it's Caribbean-colored because of suspended limestone particles, not depth. Locals call it the Caribbean of the Rockies. There's also a lake monster legend older than the state of Idaho, and it's outlasted every scientist who's tried to explain it away.
divergentPaths: The limestone particle science behind the color
The Bear Lake Monster legend — origins and modern sightings
Why this lake sits exactly on the Idaho-Utah border
Bear Lake's unique endemic fish species found nowhere else on Earth

waypoints.name: Green River / Flaming Gorge
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: The canyon opening below is 50 million years of lake sediment, cut clean by the Green River. John Wesley Powell launched his first expedition down this exact river in 1869 — one arm, no maps, heading into country nobody had charted. He didn't know if the river ended in a waterfall or an ocean.
divergentPaths: John Wesley Powell's 1869 expedition — how it actually went
The 50-million-year lake sediment record and what it preserves
Flaming Gorge Dam and reservoir — modern engineering on an ancient river
Why Powell lost an arm and how it didn't stop him

waypoints.name: Fort Bridger
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: Down there, Jim Bridger built a trading post in the middle of nowhere — and accidentally created the most important fork in the American West. The Oregon Trail, California Trail, and Mormon Trail all split apart right at Fort Bridger. Every wagon train stopped here and had to decide who they were about to become.
divergentPaths: Jim Bridger — mountain man, trapper, unlikely trail-post founder
Where the three trails actually diverge and why here
Fort Bridger's later life as a US Army outpost
How pioneers decided which trail to take at this fork

waypoints.name: South Pass
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: Out your window, the South Pass gave an erroneous sense of ease to westward travelers. This gentle saddle — nothing like a mountain pass — was the point of no return. 800 miles behind, 1,200 still ahead.
divergentPaths: Why South Pass looks nothing like a mountain pass
What travelers abandoned on the trail to lighten their loads by this point
The Continental Divide split — water flowing two different directions from here
Oregon, California, and Mormon Trail traffic through South Pass

waypoints.name: Laramie Basin Turbulence Zone
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: DYNAMIC — pull live NOAA PIREP data here

dynamicContent.turbulencePresent: The bumps you may be feeling right now have a name. The Laramie Basin is one of the most reliably turbulent corridors in commercial aviation — mountain wave action off the Medicine Bow Range. The crew knows exactly what this is. The aircraft is built for it. The fasten seatbelt sign is on because at this altitude and speed even light chop is worth respecting.
dynamicContent.turbulenceAbsent: You're in smooth air right now, but this basin — the Laramie — is one of the most reliably turbulent corridors in commercial aviation on other days. Mountain wave off the Medicine Bow Range. Today the atmosphere is cooperating.
divergentPaths: How mountain wave turbulence forms — the actual physics
PIREPs — how pilots report turbulence and how that data flows in real time
Clear air turbulence vs convective — why one is harder to predict
What the crew is actually doing right now when turbulence hits

waypoints.name: Rocky Mountain Front Range
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: You are looking at the exact fault line where the Rockies were thrust upward 70 million years ago during the Laramide orogeny. The transition from flat plains to 14,000-foot peaks with almost no foothills is geologically violent — and geologically recent. The plains were a shallow inland sea when those mountains started rising.
divergentPaths: The Laramide orogeny — what caused it and why the Rockies look different from the Appalachians
The Western Interior Seaway — the inland sea that covered Denver
Fourteeners — Colorado has 58 peaks above 14,000 feet, more than any other state
Denver's elevation paradox — the city feels flat but sits a mile above sea level

waypoints.name: Denver International Airport Final
waypoints.heading: eastbound
waypoints.hook: The white tensile roof is designed to evoke the Rockies — 34 fiberglass-coated peaks visible from miles out. The airport opened 3 years late and $2 billion over budget, spawning genuine conspiracy theories that persist today. The underground tunnels are real — they were built for an automated baggage system that never worked. The gargoyles in baggage claim are an intentional wink at the conspiracists.
divergentPaths: The DEN construction disaster — what actually went wrong with the baggage system
The conspiracy theories — Blucifer the horse, the murals, the tunnels, what people actually believe
DEN as an aviation hub — why building 25 miles from the city made operational sense
The art program — DEN has one of the largest public art collections of any airport

altitudeProfile.description: Altitude profile for SEA→DEN based on SID/STAR restrictions and typical cruise profile. Used to query PIREPs at correct flight level for each phase.

phases.name: MONTN2 SID
phases.notes: Climb out of KSEA, initial heading east

fixes.hook: You're beginning the descent into Denver. The Rockies are directly ahead — the Front Range rises from flat plains to 14,000-foot peaks with almost no foothills. That abrupt transition is geologically violent and geologically recent.

Passing through 16,000 feet over the Front Range. The plains below were a shallow inland sea 70 million years ago when these mountains started rising. Denver sits at exactly one mile above sea level — the city feels flat but you're already higher than most of the eastern US.

Through 14,000 feet — Colorado has 58 peaks above this altitude, more than any other state. The white tensile roof of Denver International is visible ahead. It was designed to evoke the Rockies. The airport opened 3 years late and $2 billion over budget.

Final approach into Denver. The gargoyles in baggage claim are an intentional wink at the conspiracy theorists. The underground tunnels are real — built for an automated baggage system that never worked.

returnRoute.notes: Flip seat side logic. C/D gets Rockies on climbout. Afternoon light on Cascades on approach is materially different from morning. Add Bonneville Dam and St. Helens crater (visible from east on westbound) as unique westbound waypoints.

uniqueWestboundWaypoints.name: Bonneville Dam
uniqueWestboundWaypoints.heading: westbound
uniqueWestboundWaypoints.hook: First major federal dam on the Columbia, completed 1937. Built by New Deal workers during the Depression. Woody Guthrie was paid $266 to write songs promoting it — he produced 26 songs in 30 days, including 'Roll On Columbia.' The dam essentially ended the Columbia as a salmon river at this point, drowning Celilo Falls and one of the oldest continuously inhabited trading sites in North America.
divergentPaths: Woody Guthrie's Columbia River songs — the commission, the songs, the politics
Celilo Falls — what was lost when the dam filled in 1957
The fish ladder debate — do they work, and what the data actually shows
New Deal infrastructure — how Bonneville fit into a larger electrification strategy

uniqueWestboundWaypoints.name: Mt. St. Helens Crater
uniqueWestboundWaypoints.heading: westbound
uniqueWestboundWaypoints.hook: From the east on this heading you can see directly into the open horseshoe crater left by the 1980 eruption. The north face of the mountain simply ceased to exist on May 18, 1980 — the largest landslide in recorded history preceded the lateral blast by seconds. 57 people died. The blast zone is still visibly recovering 45 years later.
divergentPaths: The lateral blast — why it killed people 8 miles away who thought they were safe
Harry Truman — the innkeeper who refused to leave and became a folk hero
The recovery — how ecosystems rebuild after total sterilization
David Johnston — the USGS volcanologist who died at the observation post, and what he radioed in his last seconds

interruptLogic.activeConversation: queue — surface hook at natural pause with 'By the way, we just passed something worth knowing about if you want a detour...'
interruptLogic.passengerInitiated: always respond — passenger asks 'what's that?' triggers nearest active waypoint context regardless of queue state
interruptLogic.turbulenceAlert: always immediate — overrides all queue state, never withheld

ambientContext.description: Always maintain current position context in system prompt so passenger can ask 'what's that?' at any moment and get a grounded answer

turbulenceLookahead.description: Two-layer turbulence awareness system — forecast prediction plus real-time pilot validation

layers.name: GTG Forecast — Predictive Layer
layers.logic: Query every 10 min. Filter returned polygons by current bbox extended 75nm ahead on current heading, and by FL window ±2000ft of current altitude. If hit detected, calculate intercept distance and arm turbulence hook.

layers.name: PIREP Validation — Real-Time Layer
layers.triggerCondition: GTG hit detected — fire PIREP query to validate or negate
layers.logic: Query PIREPs within 30nm of forecasted turb polygon and within last 60 minutes. Cross-reference altitude. Classify result as CONFIRM, NEGATE, or INSUFFICIENT_DATA.

hookTemplates.forecastOnly_LGT: Just a heads up — atmospheric data shows a chance of light turbulence in about {distanceNm} miles at our flight level. The crew is aware. It's nothing to be concerned about, but worth knowing.
hookTemplates.forecastOnly_MOD: Something worth knowing — we're likely to hit some moderate turbulence in roughly {distanceNm} miles. You'll feel it. The aircraft handles this routinely. This is a good moment to make sure your seatbelt is loosely fastened.
hookTemplates.confirmed_LGT: Heads up — light bumps likely in about {distanceNm} miles. Pilots ahead of us on this route are reporting it too, so it's real. Nothing dramatic.
hookTemplates.confirmed_MOD: Moderate turbulence ahead in roughly {distanceNm} miles — pilots who just flew through are confirming it. The crew knows. The aircraft is built for this. Seatbelt on.
hookTemplates.negated: The forecast showed potential turbulence ahead, but pilots who just flew through are reporting smooth air. Looks like we may get a pass today.
hookTemplates.insufficient_data: Forecast data suggests possible turbulence ahead. No recent pilot reports in the area to confirm or deny — we'll see what we find.
hookTemplates.postEvent_smooth: That was the zone the forecast flagged. Smooth through it — sometimes the atmosphere cooperates.
hookTemplates.postEvent_turb: That was the turbulence the forecast predicted. Now you know what moderate mountain wave feels like. The Laramie Basin does this regularly — it's a function of the Medicine Bow Range geometry and the prevailing westerlies.

divergentPaths: How GTG forecasts are generated — NWP models and what they're actually solving for
Why PIREPs are the ground truth and why there aren't enough of them
Mountain wave mechanics — the standing wave that causes this specific turbulence
Clear air turbulence vs convective — why CAT is harder to predict and harder to see
What the flight crew is doing right now — checklist, comms, decision tree
Turbulence intensity scale — what LGT/MOD/SEV/EXTRM actually mean structurally

dataHarvest.description: Anonymized behavioral signal collection — no PII, patterns only

aggregateReporting.tourismBoardSignal: Flag waypoints where engagement > 180 seconds as high-intent destination interest. Report by origin airport to identify feeder markets.
aggregateReporting.airlineReport: Engagement rate by route, seat class, time of day. Demonstrates IFE value proposition.
aggregateReporting.contentPerformance: Rank waypoints by engagement depth and divergent path selection. Informs content investment priorities.

stream1.name: IFE Platform Licensing
stream1.model: B2B — license to Panasonic Avionics, Thales, Collins Aerospace, or direct to airline
stream1.pitch: Differentiated IFE content that demonstrably increases passenger engagement vs standard seatback entertainment
stream1.pricing: Per-flight or per-seat-mile licensing. Negotiate through IFE vendor relationship.

stream2.name: Tourism Board Content Sponsorship
stream2.model: State and regional tourism boards sponsor waypoint depth for their geography
stream2.pitch: We put your state in front of 4M+ coastal passengers annually who would never have considered it. Here is the engagement data. Here is the intent signal. Here is conversion to actual bookings.
stream2.note: Sponsored content must maintain editorial integrity — depth and honesty are the product. No puffery.

stream3.name: Behavioral Intent Data
stream3.model: Aggregated, anonymized destination interest reports sold to tourism boards, hotel groups, travel agencies
stream3.pitch: Not demographics. Declared interest — a passenger who spent 9 minutes on Shoshone Falls self-identified. We know their origin airport. That is a conversion-ready lead cohort.
stream3.legal: Requires clear opt-in consent at session start. GDPR and CCPA compliant architecture from day one.

stream4.name: In-Moment Booking Conversion
stream4.model: Affiliate or direct partnership with travel booking platforms
stream4.pitch: Passenger engages deeply with Craters of the Moon → app surfaces 'Want to go?' → one tap to booking partner with pre-populated origin and interest context
stream4.note: Must feel organic not transactional. The moment has to earn it.

flyoverCountryThesis.description: Core editorial position — not a feature, a philosophy
flyoverCountryThesis.statement: Flyover country is the most geologically violent, historically brutal, and culturally underrepresented terrain in North America. The product does not just entertain passengers — it structurally dismantles coastal dismissiveness in real time at FL310, to exactly the audience most likely to hold it.
flyoverCountryThesis.tourismBoardPitch: We are not advertising. We are creating discovery. Nobody wants to be advertised to at 35,000 feet. Everyone wants to be surprised by what is below them. We create genuine curiosity — you just need to be there when they land.
highValueTargets: Wyoming — Shoshone Falls, Tom Horn, Laramie, Tetons
Idaho — Craters of the Moon, Snake River Canyon, Sawtooth Range
Montana — Unlimited content, near-zero current tourism marketing penetration to coastal markets
Eastern Washington — Hanford B Reactor, Columbia confluence, Palouse hills
Nevada interior — Basin and Range geology, ghost mining towns, Extraterrestrial Highway
Utah — Beyond the national parks everyone knows, the geology nobody talks about

liveDataSources.name: NOAA G-AIRMET / SIGMET Turbulence Forecast
liveDataSources.purpose: Predictive turbulence layer — forecast polygons with FL range and intensity

liveDataSources.name: NOAA Aviation Weather PIREPs
usedAt: systemwide — triggered by GTG hit for validation
liveDataSources.purpose: Real-time pilot report validation of forecast turbulence

liveDataSources.name: FlightAware AeroAPI
usedAt: systemwide — position source if IFE GPS not available
liveDataSources.purpose: Live position, altitude, heading, ground speed
